OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: team-based-primary-care-models-national-international-approaches
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: Team-based primary care models : national and international approaches : rapid review
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: As the first contact point to the healthcare system, primary care in Canada faces unprecedented pressures from provider shortages, long wait times, and the growing burden of chronic and complex conditions, leaving many patients without timely access to care. Team-based primary care, where diverse health professionals collaborate with patients and caregivers to deliver coordinated services, has emerged as a promising approach to improve access, quality, and efficiency while reducing provider burnout. The objective of this rapid review is to identify and synthesize published and grey literature on national and international models of team-based primary care (TBPC), related outcomes, and key implementation considerations, including barriers, facilitators, and access.

resources.name: Team-based primary care models : national and international approaches : rapid review

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: pet-ct-wait-time-impact-on-patient-outcome-healthcare-resource-use
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: PET/CT wait time and its impact on patient outcome, healthcare resource use, and costs in Alberta
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: The Institute of Health Economics explored a potential opportunity for mobile positron emission tomography–computed tomography (PET/CT) diagnostic imaging (DI) services for patients with lung, lymphoma, and prostate cancers in Alberta using a phased approach. A rapid review (phase 1) on use of PET/CT for this patient population was completed in January 2024. This report (phase 2) provides an assessment of travel distance and wait time for PET/CT, the association between travel distance and wait time, and the impact of wait time on patient mortality, healthcare resource use (HCRU), and costs in this patient population.

resources.name: PET/CT wait time and its impact on patient outcome, healthcare resource use, and costs in Alberta

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: alberta
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: Alberta Biomonitoring Program: Chemicals in Serum of Pregnant Women and Children in Alberta
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: Biomonitoring provides a measure of internal doses of environmental chemicals or agents and allows for a more accurate measure of human health risk from these exposures. In these Alberta Biomonitoring Program datasets, information was collected as part of 3 province-wide studies investigating the serum levels of environmental chemicals (both natural and synthetic) in pregnant women in northern, central and southern Alberta (Phase 1), in children in southern Alberta (Phase 2), and maternal and umbilical cord blood samples from 7 Alberta cities (Phase 3). The goals of the program are to create a benchmark against which to track future exposures, provide a starting point for assessing health risks, indicate possible exposure sources and prioritize research efforts. Chemicals targeted for monitoring were selected using expert guidance and review of similar studies. For Phases 1 and 2, results for each chemical are presented in a single column, as either whole serum or lipid-adjusted concentrations. Each row provides the mean concentration and standard error of measurement for the replicate pools in the Age+Region group. Supplementary rows include the Limit of Detection/Limit of Quantification (LOD/LOQ) and Comments (for report status; results are only reported if ≥25% of pooled samples had detectable concentrations of a chemical). For Phase 3, results for each chemical are presented in up to 8 columns (age-weighted and age-and-geography-weighted means in whole serum and lipid-adjusted serum, and their 95% confidence intervals (CIs)). Each row provides the mean concentration and 95% CI for the replicate pools in the Age+Region group. Supplementary rows include the LOD/LOQ, Report Status (Yes: ≥25% of pooled samples had detectable concentrations; No: <25% had detectable concentrations) and Comments. For more information, please consult the Phase 1 and 2 reports (https://open.alberta.ca/publications/9780778566953; https://open.alberta.ca/publications/9780778582786).

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: strategies-optimizing-patient-flow-through-intensive-care-unit
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: Strategies for optimizing patient flow through the Intensive Care Unit : scoping review
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: This review looks at how Canada and other places are trying to fix delays in getting patients into and out of intensive care units (ICUs). As Alberta plans to add more ICU beds, it’s important to also improve how patients move through the system. Better flow can help reduce overcrowding in emergency rooms and free up hospital beds, making sure patients get the right care at the right time. A more comprehensive evaluation of the clinical and economic effectiveness of the different strategies designed to improve ICU flow could support informed policy decisions and guide investments toward the most impactful solutions.

resources.name: Strategies for optimizing patient flow through the Intensive Care Unit : scoping review

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: PET/CT capacity for cancer diagnosis and follow-up : rapid review
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: The Institute of Health Economics explored a potential opportunity for mobile positron emission tomography–computed tomography (PET/CT) diagnostic imaging (DI) services for patients with lung, lymphoma, and prostate cancers in Alberta using a phased approach. This rapid review (phase 1) identifies clinical care pathways that involve the use of PET/CT and assesses the current evidence on the diagnostic performance and clinical and cost-effectiveness of using PET/CT in the diagnosis and follow-up of this patient population. Phase 2 (PET/CT Wait Time and Its Impact on Patient Outcome, Healthcare Resource Use, and Costs in Alberta) was completed in December 2024.

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: care-pathway-for-non-surgical-management-of-osteoarthritis
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: Care pathway for non-surgical management of osteoarthritis : a clinical and economic review
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: This health evidence review was requested by the Bone and Joint Health Strategic Clinical Network to help inform the development of a conservative care pathway for individuals with osteoarthritis in Alberta. The main objective of this review was to identify components of a clinically effective and cost-effective care pathway to improve access to and enhance delivery of non-surgical management of osteoarthritis in Alberta by addressing the following overarching policy question: What are components of an effective and sustainable care pathway for delivering non-surgical treatments to individuals with osteoarthritis?

resources.name: Care pathway for non-surgical management of osteoarthritis : a clinical and economic review

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: lga-age-standardized-inpatient-separation-rates-for-selected-conditions-2010-2011-to-2017-2018
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: Local Geographic Area (LGA) Age-Standardized Inpatient Separation (Sep) Rates (per 100,000 population) for Selected Conditions, 2010/2011 - 2017/2018
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: This figure provides the age-standardized inpatient separation rates per 100,000 population for selected conditions.  An inpatient separation from a health care facility occurs anytime a patient (or resident) leaves because of death, discharge, sign-out against medical advice or transfer. The number of separations is the most commonly used measure of the utilization of hospital services. Separations, rather than admissions, are used because hospital abstracts for inpatient care are based on information gathered at the time of discharge.  The selected conditions are Asthma, Diabetes, Influenza, Ischemic Heart Diseases, Mental and Behavioural Disorders due to Psychoactive Substance Use, Pneumonia, Pulmonary Heart and Pulmonary Circulation Diseases. Age standardization is a technique applied to make rates comparable across groups with different age distributions. A simple rate is defined as the number of people with a particular condition divided by the whole population. An age-standardized rate is defined as the number of people with a condition divided by the population within each age group. Standardizing (adjusting) the rate across age groups allows a more accurate comparison between populations that have different age structures. Age standardization is typically done when comparing rates across time periods, different geographic areas, and or population sub-groups (e.g. ethnic group). This indicator dataset contains information at both Local Geographic Area (for example, Lacombe, Red Deer, Calgary West Bow, etc.) and Alberta levels. Local geographic area refers to 132 geographic areas created by Alberta Health (AH) and Alberta Health Services (AHS) based on census boundaries. This table is the part of "Alberta Health Primary Health Care - Community Profiles" report published March 2019.

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: This table provides the age-standardized inpatient separation rates per 100,000 population for selected conditions.  An inpatient separation from a health care facility occurs anytime a patient (or resident) leaves because of death, discharge, sign-out against medical advice or transfer. The number of separations is the most commonly used measure of the utilization of hospital services. Separations, rather than admissions, are used because hospital abstracts for inpatient care are based on information gathered at the time of discharge.  The selected conditions are Asthma, Diabetes, Influenza, Ischemic Heart Diseases, Mental and Behavioural Disorders due to Psychoactive Substance Use, Pneumonia, Pulmonary Heart and Pulmonary Circulation Diseases. Age standardization is a technique applied to make rates comparable across groups with different age distributions. A simple rate is defined as the number of people with a particular condition divided by the whole population. An age-standardized rate is defined as the number of people with a condition divided by the population within each age group. Standardizing (adjusting) the rate across age groups allows a more accurate comparison between populations that have different age structures. Age standardization is typically done when comparing rates across time periods, different geographic areas, and or population sub-groups (e.g. ethnic group). This indicator dataset contains information at both Local Geographic Area (for example, Lacombe, Red Deer, Calgary West Bow, etc.) and Alberta levels. OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: continuing-care-accommodation-standards-compliance-reporting
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: Continuing Care Accommodation Standards compliance reporting
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes:
The Ministry of Assisted Living and Social Services oversees the licensing and compliance monitoring of continuing care homes and supportive living accommodations.  The Ministry assures adherence to the requirements and expectations set out in the Continuing Care Act, its regulations, and the associated accommodation and health service standards. Eligible continuing care homes and supportive living accommodations are monitored to assure compliance with these accommodation standards. 

The Accommodation Standards aim to ensure high-quality services (e.g. meals, building maintenance, security and housekeeping) that promote the safety, security and quality of life of Albertans in these living environments. Continuing care homes Type A and B are also monitored for compliance to the Continuing Care Health Service Standards, a set of legislated, minimum standards that operators must comply with in the provision of health care (e.g. medication management, care planning, staff training).

Assisted Living and Social Services currently reports publicly on the results of monitoring visits in Continuing Care Homes and Supportive Living Accommodations across the province at http://standardsandlicensing.alberta.ca.

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: l17
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: Livestock Industry Diversification Act
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes: The Livestock Industry Diversification Act establishes the framework for licensing and regulating diversified livestock farms, including diversified livestock production farms and harvest preserves. It defines diversified livestock animals and sets out requirements for licences, permits, and the operation of farms, including provisions regarding animal identification, registration, containment, and record keeping. The Act also addresses the possession, transportation, importation and exportation of diversified livestock animals, and includes provisions governing harvesting on harvest preserves and related restrictions on use and commerce. The Act provides for inspection and enforcement, including offences and penalties, and enables regulations related to species eligibility, farm operations, animal health and care, and the management of strays.

resources.name: Livestock Industry Diversification Act

OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.name: domestic-well-water-quality-in-alberta-routine-chemistry
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.title: Domestic Well Water Quality in Alberta - Routine Chemistry
OPEN_ALBERTA_PACKAGES.notes:
In rural Alberta, 90 per cent of people use private well water supplies for domestic use (e.g., drinking, cooking, bathing). Domestic well water systems are not regulated by the provincial or federal governments. The Government of Alberta along with Alberta Health Services (AHS) provides water chemistry testing of private well water and information and advice on safe water for domestic purposes; however, it is the responsibility of well owners to ensure the quality and safety of their water supply. Water quality may be impacted by contamination from natural sources or human activities and cause noticeable aesthetic issues or potential health concerns.
Water samples are collected and submitted by well owners through local AHS sites for analysis of routine chemistry and trace element parameters. Routine chemistry testing focuses on the suitability of the water for drinking and household use with two health-related parameters. For trace elements, testing used to be conducted only when there were health concerns or when the water was suspected to contain chemicals of concern (2001 to Sep 2018). Currently, trace element testing is completed for all samples submitted for routine analysis (if the sample volume is sufficient). The Alberta Centre for Toxicology has conducted the analyses of raw domestic well water samples since March 2004. From 2001 to Mar 2004, testing was conducted by Enviro-Test Laboratories. Limited information is available regarding the analytical methods and detection limits for this lab; therefore, users are advised to exercise caution when using the 2001 to Mar 2004 data.
These datasets contain the routine chemistry results for raw well water samples collected from 2001 to 2020. Corrections may be made to the dataset over time (e.g., removal of samples deemed to be treated); users should regularly check for updates and download the most current versions.
For additional information, refer to the publications on the “Related” tab of this webpage.
